plot_anchor: Katje, Blicero, and Gottfried form one of the book's first sustained erotic-political theatres.
interpretive_note: The early Rocket world is already intimate: sexuality, occupation, surveillance, and colonial fantasy bind the weapon to domination before it becomes a technical object.

plot_anchor: Roger and Jessica briefly hold a private wartime romance against the pressure of the systems around them.
interpretive_note: Their episode gives the score one of its clearest counterweights: ordinary attachment becomes legible only because the war machine keeps pressing on it.

plot_anchor: Franz Pökler, Leni, Ilse, Rathenau, and IG Farben pull family history into the industrial prehistory of the Rocket.
interpretive_note: The Pökler line starts before the Zone: domestic injury, chemical capitalism, and engineering obedience are already becoming one apparatus.

plot_anchor: Slothrop moves through the Riviera casino world while Katje, Tantivy, Bloat, and intelligence networks close around him.
interpretive_note: The casino sequence turns glamour into machinery: pursuit, sexual comedy, and intelligence work are cut into a social field of observation.

plot_anchor: Slothrop's casino intimacy with Katje turns into loss, uncertainty, and further manipulation by hidden agencies.
interpretive_note: Absence becomes a plot engine here: erotic farce gives way to paranoia because the systems around Slothrop can use disappearance as control.

plot_anchor: The Imipolex G/Jamf material clarifies that Slothrop's quest is tied to a specific plastic, chemical genealogy, and corporate archive.
interpretive_note: This is where the Rocket stops being only symbol or target and becomes material system: polymer, file, laboratory, corporation, and childhood conditioning.

plot_anchor: The book enters the Zone as Slothrop, Tchitcherine, Geli, and scattered military remnants move through the postwar landscape.
interpretive_note: Part III opens by changing the reader's footing: freedom, ruin, erotic possibility, pursuit, and sign-reading arrive together rather than in sequence.

plot_anchor: Tchitcherine's Central Asian history expands the Rocket quest into Soviet, colonial, linguistic, and fraternal conflict.
interpretive_note: The Zone becomes global here: the Rocket system is mirrored by empire, ethnography, intelligence, and family rupture rather than confined to Germany.

plot_anchor: Franz Pökler's relation to Ilse, Weissmann, Dora, and the Rocket becomes one of the novel's central moral exposures.
interpretive_note: The longest formal unit is not merely expansive; it concentrates the book's problem of technical obedience, familial desire, and historical guilt.

plot_anchor: Slothrop is carried deeper into the maritime and theatrical drift of the Zone, where identities and allegiances become increasingly unstable.
interpretive_note: This anchor marks the Zone as a stage for unstable crossings: the formal density matches a world of quick performances, masks, and dissolving roles.

plot_anchor: Springer, Slothrop, the Anubis material, and film-like staging turn the Zone into a media apparatus as much as a geography.
interpretive_note: The episode makes plot feel edited and projected: the Rocket system now includes cameras, performances, and control over the terms of appearance.

plot_anchor: Bodine, Marvy, Krypton, and Slothrop/Rocketman mark the late Zone's mixture of military farce, erotic drift, and identity breakdown.
interpretive_note: Slothrop's line is no longer a stable quest line; it disperses into roles, jokes, uniforms, rumours, and late-Zone farce.

plot_anchor: Byron the Bulb turns industrial infrastructure into a comic-political allegory of power, communication, and planned obsolescence.
interpretive_note: Byron matters because the novel's systems are no longer only human networks: technology itself becomes political, comic, and strangely animate.

plot_anchor: Slothrop, Bodine, pop-cultural fragments, racial fantasy, technical jargon, and comic detours spread the late novel into a centrifugal field.
interpretive_note: This late anchor shows form following dissolution: Slothrop's plot line is distributed through American noise, technical language, and cultural fragments.

plot_anchor: Tchitcherine is pulled back towards Central Asia, where return reads less like resolution than operational extinction.
interpretive_note: A counter-quest closes without revelation: history folds around the pursuer rather than yielding the object pursued.

plot_anchor: The finale brings theatre, Rocket, Gottfried, Blicero/Weissmann, Slothrop traces, tarot, song, and apocalypse into one terminal convergence.
interpretive_note: The closing movement stops separating plot, ritual, cinema, theology, and weapon trajectory: the Rocket becomes event, theatre, and end-time apparatus at once.
